content.text: Choosing
which few-shot demonstrations

to include in a prompt is one of the highest-leverage decisions in in-context learning. A small set of carefully selected examples can push a language model to near fine-tuned accuracy on a task, while a poorly chosen set—or a static set that cannot adapt to diverse queries—can actively harm performance. In agentic systems, where the distribution of incoming tasks is wide and unpredictable, dynamic example selection is therefore a first-class engineering concern.
What it is

Example selection is the process of algorithmically choosing a subset of k demonstrations from a larger pool to include in a few-shot prompt. Rather than hard-coding a fixed list of examples at prompt-design time, a selection algorithm runs at query time and returns the examples that are most likely to help the model produce a correct output for that specific input.
A few-shot
demonstration

is a complete (input, output) pair that illustrates the task. When a model sees several demonstrations before the actual query, it can infer the task format, the expected output structure, the reasoning style, and edge-case handling—without any parameter update. This is the essence of in-context learning. Example selection controls the quality of that inference.

Selection strategies range from trivially simple (random sampling) to sophisticated retrieval systems backed by vector databases. The four principal families are semantic similarity, diversity sampling, difficulty matching, and error-driven or adaptive selection. Each makes a different assumption about what property of an example makes it most useful.
Why it matters

Empirical studies consistently show that the choice of examples matters more than the number of examples. Adding a fourth example that is irrelevant to the query can degrade performance relative to using three well-chosen examples. This is counterintuitive: more context should help, yet irrelevant context introduces misleading surface patterns that the model may latch onto.

Agentic pipelines amplify this sensitivity. A customer-support agent receives queries spanning billing disputes, technical faults, product returns, and account access—all in the same session. A static set of three examples cannot adequately cover this breadth. Without dynamic selection, the model frequently produces responses that follow the structure of the wrong example type, leading to hallucinated fields, wrong classification labels, or malformed tool-call arguments.

Context-window costs are a second pressure. Tokens are expensive and bounded. A prompt that uses all available space for maximally informative examples achieves better throughput than one that wastes tokens on irrelevant demonstrations. Selection also enables a controlled trade-off: a similarity threshold can allow zero examples when no good match exists, saving tokens entirely, instead of defaulting to poor ones.
Benefits
Higher task accuracy.
Similarity-based retrieval consistently outperforms random selection, often by 5–15 percentage points on classification and extraction benchmarks.
Generalisation across input distributions.
Dynamic selection adapts to every query, so the system does not need a separate prompt for each input sub-type—one selection algorithm handles them all.
Context-window efficiency.
Examples are chosen for relevance, so fewer tokens are wasted on demonstrations that do not apply to the current query.
Self-improving with error-driven feedback.

An adaptive selector can up-weight examples that historically correlated with correct outputs and down-weight examples linked to failures, making the system improve over time without retraining the model.
Decoupled example curation from prompt engineering.
The example bank is a separate, versioned artefact. New examples can be added or bad ones removed without editing prompt templates.
Trade-offs

The primary cost of dynamic selection is latency and operational complexity. A nearest-neighbour lookup against a large example bank adds a retrieval step to every request. For sub-100ms SLA APIs this can be prohibitive without a caching layer. The embedding model itself must be run on the query—if a large sentence-transformer is used inline this can add 50–200 ms of CPU time.

Example quality becomes a hard dependency. If the example bank is contaminated with incorrectly labelled or low-quality demonstrations, selection will faithfully retrieve bad examples—garbage in, garbage out. Curating and auditing the bank is an ongoing operational cost with no obvious endpoint.
Similarity is not the same as correctness. Retrieving the most similar example is only useful if that example is correct—a similar but wrong demonstration actively misleads the model.

Coverage gaps. If the example bank lacks coverage of an input type, the best available example may still be a poor match. Monitoring retrieval similarity distributions exposes these gaps but does not automatically fill them.

Ordering sensitivity. The sequence in which selected examples are presented matters. Models tend to follow the pattern set by the last example most strongly (recency bias). Suboptimal ordering can negate gains from good selection.
Selection strategy hyperparameters (k, λ, similarity threshold) require tuning on held-out data. Without proper evaluation they are guesses.
How it works

All selection strategies share a two-phase structure: an offline indexing phase and an online retrieval phase. In the offline phase, every example in the bank is embedded into a dense vector and indexed in a data structure that supports fast nearest-neighbour queries. In the online phase, the incoming query is embedded and the index is searched for the top-k matches according to a scoring function.
Semantic similarity selection
The most common strategy computes the cosine similarity between the query embedding
and each example embedding

When embeddings are pre-normalised to unit length, the cosine similarity reduces to a dot product and can be computed with a single matrix-vector multiplication against the entire bank. The top-k examples by score are selected. BM25 term-frequency scoring is an alternative when sparse lexical overlap is more predictive than dense semantics—common in domains with specialised vocabulary.
Max-Marginal Relevance (MMR)

Pure similarity selection can return a set of k nearly identical examples—all semantically close to the query but redundant with each other. Max-Marginal Relevance addresses this by penalising examples that are similar to already-selected ones:
where
is the set of already-selected examples and

controls the relevance-diversity trade-off. MMR is executed greedily: at each step the next example that maximises the MMR score given the current selection is added. This produces a set that is collectively more informative than a purely similarity-ranked subset.
Difficulty matching

For reasoning-heavy tasks, examples should match not just topic but also difficulty. An example that requires the same number of reasoning steps, involves a similarly complex entity structure, or uses the same tool sequence as the target query is more useful than a topically similar but trivially simple one. Difficulty signals can be derived from output length, chain-of-thought depth, or a small classifier trained on annotated complexity labels.
Error-driven and adaptive selection

An adaptive selector augments similarity scores with a reliability signal derived from past outcomes. After each LLM invocation the system records whether the output was correct (verified against a ground truth, a downstream tool call, or a human label). Examples that appeared in prompts that led to correct outputs receive an increased weight; examples linked to failures receive a penalty. Over time the selector biases toward demonstrations that empirically improve accuracy.
Context-window budget constraint
Regardless of strategy, the selected set must fit within the available context. Let
be the total context-window capacity in tokens,
the system-prompt token count, and
the query token count. The feasibility constraint is:
A token-aware selector computes a cumulative token count and stops adding examples once the budget is exhausted, even if
examples were requested.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Example selection pipeline: query enters at top, is matched against an example pool via four strategies inside a selection engine, top-k examples flow to a prompt builder, which feeds the LLM; a dashed feedback loop carries error signals back to the pool for adaptive reweighting.

content.text: The pipeline shows two offline components (Example Pool and Embedding Store) and three online components (Selection Engine, Prompt Builder, LLM). Pre-indexed vectors in FAISS or a similar ANN structure allow sub-millisecond nearest-neighbour lookups even for banks of hundreds of thousands of examples. The feedback loop on the right is optional but enables error-driven adaptation without retraining the language model.
When to use it

Dynamic example selection is most valuable when: the task input distribution is broad and heterogeneous; the prompt budget is limited enough that every example must earn its place; or the task requires precise formatting that the model learns best by imitation rather than instruction.
Classification and extraction tasks with many categories—retrieval covers the long tail without exhaustive prompt enumeration.
Agentic tool-call generation, where the argument schema varies by tool and a relevant worked example dramatically reduces malformed calls.
Few-shot reasoning chains where the structure of the chain must match the query's complexity level.
Multi-tenant APIs that serve many customers, each with distinct domain vocabulary and task types, from a single model deployment.

Dynamic selection is less appropriate when: the task is extremely uniform (every query is structurally identical); the example bank is too small to offer meaningful diversity; latency budgets cannot accommodate a retrieval step; or the deployment environment lacks the infrastructure to host a vector store. In these cases a hand-curated static prompt is simpler and may perform comparably.
How to implement
A production implementation has three layers: bank management, indexing, and query-time retrieval.
Step 1 — Curate and store the example bank

Begin by collecting (input, output) pairs from verified sources: human annotations, accepted model outputs reviewed by domain experts, or held-out test-set examples with known labels. Store them in a structured format with unique IDs and metadata (label, difficulty tier, date added, source). Exclude examples with label noise.
Step 2 — Pre-compute and index embeddings
Choose an embedding model appropriate for the domain.
all-MiniLM-L6-v2

(22 M parameters) is fast for English general-purpose tasks. Larger domain-specific models (e.g. legal, biomedical) improve recall in specialist settings. Embed every example input at bank-build time, not at query time. Store vectors in a FAISS flat or IVF index, or a managed vector store such as Chroma, Pinecone, or pgvector.
Step 3 — Query-time selection

On each incoming request: embed the query, run the ANN lookup, apply any reranking (MMR, difficulty filter, budget constraint), assemble the prompt in the format the model expects, and call the LLM. Log which examples were used, the prompt token count, and the outcome so you can drive adaptive reweighting.

# Simple example: semantic similarity-based example selection
# Requires: pip install sentence-transformers numpy scikit-learn

import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

# --- Example bank -----------------------------------------------------------
examples = [
    {"input": "What is the capital of France?",       "output": "Paris"},
    {"input": "What is the capital of Germany?",      "output": "Berlin"},
    {"input": "What is the capital of Japan?",        "output": "Tokyo"},
    {"input": "What is the largest planet?",          "output": "Jupiter"},
    {"input": "Who wrote Romeo and Juliet?",          "output": "Shakespeare"},
    {"input": "What is the boiling point of water?",  "output": "100 °C at sea level"},
    {"input": "What is the speed of light?",          "output": "299,792,458 m/s"},
]

# Pre-compute embeddings once and cache them
example_texts = [e["input"] for e in examples]
example_embs  = model.encode(example_texts, normalize_embeddings=True)

def select_examples(query: str, k: int = 3) -> list[dict]:
    """Return the k most semantically similar examples for a query."""
    query_emb = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True)
    sims      = cosine_similarity(query_emb, example_embs)[0]
    top_k     = np.argsort(sims)[::-1][:k]
    return [examples[i] for i in top_k]

def build_prompt(query: str, k: int = 3) -> str:
    selected = select_examples(query, k)
    lines = []
    for i, ex in enumerate(selected, 1):
        lines.append(f"Example {i}:")
        lines.append(f"  Q: {ex['input']}")
        lines.append(f"  A: {ex['output']}")
        lines.append("")
    lines.append(f"Q: {query}")
    lines.append("A:")
    return "\n".join(lines)

# --- Test -------------------------------------------------------------------
query = "What is the capital of Italy?"
print(build_prompt(query, k=2))
# Example 1:
#   Q: What is the capital of Germany?
#   A: Berlin
#
# Example 2:
#   Q: What is the capital of France?
#   A: Paris
#
# Q: What is the capital of Italy?
# A:
Examples
Example 1 — MMR selector with diversity control
The following implementation builds an MMR-based selector from scratch using only NumPy. It can be dropped into any project without framework dependencies and is straightforward to test and extend.

# Max-Marginal Relevance (MMR) example selector — balances relevance and diversity
# Requires: pip install numpy

import numpy as np
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable, Optional
import hashlib


@dataclass
class Example:
    id: str
    input: str
    output: str
    metadata: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    embedding: Optional[np.ndarray] = None


class MMRExampleSelector:
    """
    Example selector implementing Max-Marginal Relevance (MMR).

    MMR selects examples that are simultaneously relevant to the query
    and diverse among themselves, avoiding redundant demonstrations.

    Args:
        embed_fn:  callable(text: str) -> list[float]
        lam:       balance parameter λ in [0, 1].
                   1.0 = pure relevance (same as cosine-similarity ranking)
                   0.0 = pure diversity (maximise spread, ignore relevance)
                   0.5 = balanced (recommended default)
    """

    def __init__(self, embed_fn: Callable, lam: float = 0.5):
        self.embed_fn = embed_fn
        self.lam = lam
        self._examples: list[Example] = []
        self._emb_cache: dict[str, np.ndarray] = {}

    # ── Bank management ───────────────────────────────────────────────────

    def add_example(self, input_text: str, output_text: str,
                    metadata: dict | None = None) -> str:
        """Add an example; pre-compute and cache its embedding."""
        ex_id     = hashlib.sha256(input_text.encode()).hexdigest()[:10]
        embedding = self._embed(input_text)
        self._examples.append(Example(
            id=ex_id, input=input_text, output=output_text,
            metadata=metadata or {}, embedding=embedding,
        ))
        return ex_id

    def _embed(self, text: str) -> np.ndarray:
        key = hashlib.sha256(text.encode()).hexdigest()
        if key not in self._emb_cache:
            raw = np.array(self.embed_fn(text), dtype=np.float32)
            norm = np.linalg.norm(raw)
            self._emb_cache[key] = raw / (norm + 1e-9)
        return self._emb_cache[key]

    @staticmethod
    def _cos(a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
        return float(np.dot(a, b))   # vectors are pre-normalised

    # ── Selection ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    def select(self, query: str, k: int = 4) -> list[Example]:
        """Select k examples via MMR."""
        if not self._examples:
            return []
        k = min(k, len(self._examples))
        q_emb = self._embed(query)
        return self._mmr(q_emb, k)

    def _mmr(self, q_emb: np.ndarray, k: int) -> list[Example]:
        lam       = self.lam
        candidates = list(range(len(self._examples)))
        selected   = []

        for _ in range(k):
            best_idx   = -1
            best_score = float("-inf")

            for idx in candidates:
                ex        = self._examples[idx]
                relevance = self._cos(q_emb, ex.embedding)

                if selected:
                    redundancy = max(
                        self._cos(ex.embedding, self._examples[s].embedding)
                        for s in selected
                    )
                else:
                    redundancy = 0.0

                score = lam * relevance - (1.0 - lam) * redundancy
                if score > best_score:
                    best_score = score
                    best_idx   = idx

            selected.append(best_idx)
            candidates.remove(best_idx)

        return [self._examples[i] for i in selected]

    # ── Prompt building ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    def build_prompt(self, query: str, k: int = 4, system: str = "") -> str:
        """Assemble a few-shot prompt with MMR-selected examples."""
        examples = self.select(query, k)
        parts = []
        if system:
            parts.append(f"System: {system}\n")
        for i, ex in enumerate(examples, 1):
            parts.append(f"Example {i}:")
            parts.append(f"  Input:  {ex.input}")
            parts.append(f"  Output: {ex.output}")
            parts.append("")
        parts.append(f"Input:  {query}")
        parts.append("Output:")
        return "\n".join(parts)


# ── Usage example ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def mock_embed(text: str) -> list[float]:
    """Deterministic mock embedding — replace with a real model in production."""
    h   = int(hashlib.md5(text.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
    rng = np.random.default_rng(h % (2 ** 32))
    return rng.standard_normal(64).tolist()

selector = MMRExampleSelector(embed_fn=mock_embed, lam=0.6)

bank = [
    ("Classify this email as spam or ham",
     "Label: ham  Reason: personal message with specific context"),
    ("Is this customer review positive or negative?",
     "Label: positive  Reason: uses praise words and recommends product"),
    ("Detect the sentiment of this tweet",
     "Sentiment: negative  Score: 0.82"),
    ("Categorise this support ticket by department",
     "Department: billing  Priority: high"),
    ("Extract the product name from this review",
     "Product: UltraBoost Pro 2024"),
    ("Summarise the key complaint in one sentence",
     "Complaint: delayed delivery with no status update from courier"),
]

for inp, out in bank:
    selector.add_example(inp, out)

query = "Identify whether this product review expresses satisfaction or frustration"
print(selector.build_prompt(query, k=3, system="You are a text classification assistant."))
Example 2 — Adaptive (error-driven) selector for agentic loops

This pattern wraps similarity selection with a running success-rate score. After each LLM call the agent records the outcome; the selector progressively up-weights examples that empirically reduce errors.

# Error-driven adaptive selector: up-weights examples that correlate with success.

import json, math, hashlib
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class AnnotatedExample:
    input: str
    output: str
    embedding: list[float]
    failure_count: int = 0
    success_count: int = 0

class AdaptiveExampleSelector:
    """
    Blends cosine similarity with a running success-rate score.
    alpha=0.3 means 70 % similarity + 30 % historical reliability.
    """
    def __init__(self, embed_fn: Callable, alpha: float = 0.3):
        self.embed_fn = embed_fn
        self.alpha    = alpha
        self._bank: list[AnnotatedExample] = []

    def add(self, input_text: str, output_text: str) -> None:
        emb = self.embed_fn(input_text)
        self._bank.append(AnnotatedExample(input=input_text, output=output_text,
                                            embedding=emb))

    @staticmethod
    def _cos(a: list[float], b: list[float]) -> float:
        dot  = sum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))
        norm = math.sqrt(sum(x**2 for x in a)) * math.sqrt(sum(x**2 for x in b))
        return dot / (norm + 1e-9)

    def _reliability(self, ex: AnnotatedExample) -> float:
        total = ex.success_count + ex.failure_count
        return ex.success_count / total if total else 0.5

    def select(self, query: str, k: int = 3) -> list[AnnotatedExample]:
        q_emb = self.embed_fn(query)
        scored = sorted(
            self._bank,
            key=lambda ex: (1 - self.alpha) * self._cos(q_emb, ex.embedding)
                          + self.alpha * self._reliability(ex),
            reverse=True,
        )
        return scored[:k]

    def record_outcome(self, used: list[AnnotatedExample], success: bool) -> None:
        """Call after each LLM response to update reliability weights."""
        for ex in used:
            if success: ex.success_count += 1
            else:       ex.failure_count += 1

    def build_prompt(self, query: str, k: int = 3) -> tuple[str, list[AnnotatedExample]]:
        selected = self.select(query, k)
        lines = ["You are a JSON extraction assistant.", ""]
        for i, ex in enumerate(selected, 1):
            lines += [f"# Example {i}", f"Input:  {ex.input}", f"Output: {ex.output}", ""]
        lines += ["# Task", f"Input:  {query}", "Output:"]
        return "\n".join(lines), selected

# ── Usage ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def fake_embed(text: str) -> list[float]:
    h = int(hashlib.md5(text.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
    return [math.sin(h * i * 0.001) for i in range(32)]

selector = AdaptiveExampleSelector(embed_fn=fake_embed, alpha=0.25)
for inp, out in [
    ('Extract name/role: "Bob is our lead designer."', '{"name":"Bob","role":"designer"}'),
    ('Extract name/role: "Carol joined as PM."',       '{"name":"Carol","role":"PM"}'),
    ('Extract name/role: "Dave is an SWE."',           '{"name":"Dave","role":"SWE"}'),
]:
    selector.add(inp, out)

query = 'Extract name/role: "Eve leads the data science team."'
prompt, used = selector.build_prompt(query, k=2)
response = '{"name":"Eve","role":"data science lead"}'   # simulated LLM output
try:
    json.loads(response)
    selector.record_outcome(used, success=True)
    print("OK →", response)
except json.JSONDecodeError:
    selector.record_outcome(used, success=False)
Real-world example

A multi-tenant customer-support platform routes tickets to an LLM that classifies intent (billing, technical, returns, general) and drafts a reply. The platform receives 50,000 tickets per day across 200 enterprise customers, each with a distinct product vocabulary and escalation policy.

A static few-shot prompt covering all four intent categories required 12 examples to maintain acceptable accuracy, consuming ~1,200 tokens per request. Switching to semantic similarity retrieval against a bank of 3,000 curated examples (embedded offline and stored in FAISS) reduced the required examples to 3 per request (~300 tokens), cutting prompt token cost by 75% while improving classification F1 from 0.83 to 0.91. The latency added by the FAISS lookup (P99: 6 ms) was negligible compared to the LLM call (P99: 1,200 ms).

An error-driven layer added six weeks later treated escalated tickets as failures and penalised examples linked to them. After 30 days the escalation rate fell from 11% to 7% with no model retraining.
Common mistakes
Retrieving by input similarity and ignoring output quality

A selector that finds the most similar input but ignores output correctness will retrieve confidently wrong demonstrations. Always ensure the example bank is curated from verified, correct outputs. A similarity score says nothing about whether the output in the retrieved example is right.
Returning too many examples

Stuffing the context with every available example backfires. More examples increase distraction, raise token costs, and can push the system prompt or query out of the effective attention window. Benchmark with k = 1, 2, 3, 4 and pick the k that maximises accuracy per token on a held-out validation set.
Using the embedding model as a proxy for task fit
General-purpose embedding models (trained on web text) are not calibrated for task-specific notions of similarity. Two customer-support tickets may embed closely in
all-MiniLM
space yet belong to different intent categories. For narrow domains, fine-tune the embedding model on in-domain data or add a reranking step that uses a task-specific classifier.
Ignoring example ordering

Models exhibit recency bias: the last few-shot example before the query has the strongest influence. If the most relevant example is placed first and a moderately relevant one last, performance degrades. Either order examples by ascending relevance (most relevant last) or run ablation experiments to determine the best ordering for the specific model and task.
Static bank without monitoring

The example bank drifts out of distribution as the real-world input distribution evolves. A ticket-support bot trained on 2023 examples may not cover queries about products launched in 2025. Schedule regular audits: measure the distribution of top-1 retrieval similarity scores; a declining median indicates coverage gaps that require new examples to be added.
Best practices
Separate bank management from prompt logic.
Treat the example bank as a first-class artefact with its own versioning, tests, and deployment pipeline.
Pre-normalise embeddings.
Normalising vectors to unit length at index-build time reduces the cosine similarity computation to a dot product, halving computation cost.
Cap k by token budget, not by a fixed number.
Compute cumulative token count greedily and stop adding examples at the budget boundary. This prevents context overflow on long examples.
Use MMR or a diversity filter for broad-coverage tasks.
Topically redundant examples waste the context budget without adding new information.
Log which examples were used in every prompt.
This is the prerequisite for error-driven adaptation, coverage analysis, and debugging unexpected outputs.
Validate new examples before adding them to the bank.
Run a smoke test: prompt the model with only the candidate example and verify that the correct output is produced.
Order examples by ascending relevance (most relevant last).
This aligns with the model's recency bias and typically improves task performance.
Monitor retrieval similarity distributions in production.
Alert when median top-1 similarity falls below a threshold—it signals a distribution shift requiring new examples.
Performance & scalability

The dominant cost is the embedding lookup. For a bank of N examples with embedding dimension d, an exact nearest-neighbour search costs O(Nd) per query. FAISS flat indices handle this efficiently in RAM for banks up to ~1M examples. Beyond that, approximate nearest-neighbour structures (IVF, HNSW) reduce search time to O(log N) or O(√N) at the cost of a small recall penalty.
Query embedding is the second bottleneck. A
all-MiniLM-L6-v2

model running on CPU embeds a typical query in 10–30 ms. Batching multiple concurrent queries and running on GPU reduces this to under 5 ms per query at high throughput. Caching embeddings for repeated queries (keyed by query hash) eliminates the cost entirely for common inputs.
Small banks (N < 10,000):
flat index in RAM, exact search, negligible latency.
Medium banks (10K–500K):
FAISS IVFFlat or Chroma with ANN, P99 < 10 ms.
Large banks (> 500K):
FAISS HNSW or a managed vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate) with distributed search.
Multi-tenant:
partition the index by tenant to prevent cross-contamination of examples and allow per-tenant example curation.

MMR selection is O(k²) in the number of selected examples because it computes pairwise similarities among the selected set at each step. For k ≤ 10 this is negligible; for larger k consider a greedy clustering step that groups candidates and then picks the top representative from each cluster.
Tooling & ecosystem
LangChain provides
SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector
and
MaxMarginalRelevanceExampleSelector
that plug directly into
FewShotPromptTemplate
. Both support any LangChain-compatible vector store as the backend.
LlamaIndex exposes
ExampleSelectorQueryEngine

, which integrates with its retrieval and node-postprocessor pipeline. FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is the standard open-source indexing library; for managed scale, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, and pgvector (PostgreSQL extension) all support semantic search with metadata filtering.

# Using LangChain's SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector with a real vector store
# Requires: pip install langchain langchain-openai faiss-cpu

from langchain_core.prompts import FewShotPromptTemplate, PromptTemplate
from langchain_core.example_selectors import SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS

examples = [
    {"question": "Who is Albert Einstein?",
     "answer": "A theoretical physicist best known for the theory of relativity."},
    {"question": "What is photosynthesis?",
     "answer": "The process plants use to convert sunlight into glucose."},
    {"question": "Who invented the telephone?",
     "answer": "Alexander Graham Bell, patented in 1876."},
    {"question": "What is the Pythagorean theorem?",
     "answer": "a² + b² = c², relating the sides of a right-angled triangle."},
]

# Build the selector backed by FAISS
selector = SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector.from_examples(
    examples,
    OpenAIEmbeddings(),
    FAISS,
    k=2,
)

example_prompt = PromptTemplate(
    input_variables=["question", "answer"],
    template="Question: {question}\nAnswer: {answer}",
)

few_shot_prompt = FewShotPromptTemplate(
    example_selector=selector,
    example_prompt=example_prompt,
    suffix="Question: {input}\nAnswer:",
    input_variables=["input"],
)

# At query time, the two most similar examples are retrieved automatically
print(few_shot_prompt.format(input="Who discovered gravity?"))
sentence-transformers
— open-source models for high-quality sentence embeddings.
FAISS
— efficient CPU/GPU exact and approximate NN search.
Chroma
— lightweight embedded vector database, ideal for prototyping.
LangChain
— end-to-end wrappers with built-in few-shot selectors.
LlamaIndex
— retrieval-augmented generation pipeline with example-selection primitives.
Pinecone / Weaviate
— managed vector databases with low-latency ANN at scale.
Comparison with alternatives
The table below compares the principal approaches to providing task demonstrations to a language model:

Low — requires retraining on shift
Very high

Fine-tuning achieves the highest ceiling but requires labelled data, GPU compute, and a re-deployment cycle for every distribution shift. Dynamic example selection delivers most of the accuracy gain with near-zero training cost, making it the preferred first step before committing to fine-tuning.
Security & reliability

Example banks introduce a new attack surface: prompt-injection via malicious examples. If an adversary can insert a crafted (input, output) pair into the bank—for instance by submitting a ticket that contains instruction text as its output—that pair may be retrieved and included in future prompts, steering the model toward unintended behaviour.

Mitigations include: validating all candidate examples against an allowlist of safe output patterns before admission; running new examples through an LLM-based safety classifier; requiring human approval for examples above a suspicion score; and monitoring the distribution of outputs from prompts that used each example to detect sudden shifts.
Validate example outputs structurally.
For JSON extraction tasks, assert that every output in the bank is valid JSON and conforms to the expected schema before adding it.
Separate write access to the bank from production retrieval.
The production retriever should have read-only access. Example addition goes through a separate review pipeline.
Handle retrieval failures gracefully.
If the vector store is unavailable, fall back to a hardcoded minimal static prompt rather than sending a zero-shot request that may fail silently.
Log and alert on anomalous similarity distributions.
A sudden spike in very high retrieval similarity scores across many queries may indicate that a single fabricated example has been added and is being selected repeatedly.
Retire stale examples.
Examples referencing deprecated APIs, old product names, or superseded policies should be removed to prevent the model from producing outdated outputs.
Key takeaways
Example quality and relevance matter more than example count—a single well-matched demonstration often outperforms four weakly relevant ones.
Dynamic retrieval via semantic similarity (cosine or BM25) is the baseline that nearly every production few-shot system should implement before tuning anything else.
Max-Marginal Relevance corrects the redundancy problem inherent in pure similarity ranking; use it whenever k > 2 and the input space is broad.
Pre-compute and cache example embeddings offline; the only per-request work should be the query embedding and the ANN lookup.
Enforce a token-budget constraint so that k is determined by available context, not by a fixed number that can overflow the window.
Error-driven adaptation turns the selection layer into a self-improving component that upgrades accuracy over time without model retraining.
Treat the example bank as a versioned, audited artefact—validate examples before admission, monitor retrieval distributions in production, and retire stale examples on a schedule.
Dynamic example selection is the lowest-cost path to high few-shot accuracy: it delivers most of the benefit of fine-tuning with a fraction of the engineering effort and without retraining the model.
